first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h SOS for fever >100F, drops/syrup
0.65% normal saline, 2-3 drops each nostril 4-6 times/day
Antihistamine-decongestant drops, dose as per weight, TDS, max 5 days. Use cautiously in infants
Add in warm water or inhalation cloth, 2-3 times/day for congestion relief
Honey (>1 year only) + warm fluids. Avoid strong cough syrups in infants

notes: Viral illness, self-limiting 5-7 days. No routine antibiotics. Symptomatic management is mainstay. Honey for cough (>1 year only). Steam/menthol inhalation 2-3 times/day for congestion relief. Antibiotics only if secondary bacterial infection suspected.
source: IAP 2024, WHO 2023, ACCP 2017
guideline_changes: IAP 2024: Continued emphasis on no routine antibiotics or OTC cough/cold medicines in children; ACCP: Recommends against OTC cough suppressants in pediatric URI; No major guideline changes in 2024-2025 for common cold management
counselling: This is a viral illness - antibiotics are not needed
Medicines are only for symptom relief, not to cure the cold
Continue breastfeeding / ORS / fluids frequently
Continue feeding with small frequent feeds
Keep child warm, avoid cold exposure
Avoid over-medication
Report immediately if fast breathing or chest retractions
Report immediately if poor feeding or persistent vomiting
Report immediately if lethargy or irritability
Report immediately if fever persists >3 days
Report immediately if oxygen saturation drops
referral_criteria: Refer if persistent high fever >3 days, toxic appearance, or suspicion of bacterial complication
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe respiratory distress, poor feeding, dehydration, or toxic appearance

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Persistent high fever >3 days or toxic look

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or refusal to eat
Fever not responding to medication for more than 3 days

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 ml (10 drops) once daily PO, contains Vitamins A, B-complex, C, D. Continue till 1 year of age
2-3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO once daily, start 2-4 weeks after birth, continue till 6-12 months corrected age
400 IU/day PO once daily, continue at least 1 year
50-75 mg/kg/day elemental calcium PO once daily, for bone mineralization and prevention of osteopenia of prematurity
1-5 ml/day divided with feeds PO, for increased caloric intake and weight gain
5-6 drops SOS PO, only if symptomatic infantile colic. Avoid routine use
Pediasure or preterm formula if poor weight gain, 1/7 scoop per extra feed. Prefer breastfeeding first

notes: Goals: adequate weight gain (15-20 g/kg/day), prevent nutritional deficiencies (iron, calcium, Vitamin D), monitor neurodevelopment, prevent anemia/rickets/infections. Exclusive breastfeeding till 6 months corrected age, feeds every 2-3 hours. Fortified feeds if poor weight gain. Use CORRECTED age for growth/development, CHRONOLOGICAL age for vaccinations. Catch-up growth expected by 1-2 years.
source: IAP 2024 Neonatal Guidelines, WHO 2022 Preterm Care Recommendations, AAP 2023
guideline_changes: IAP 2025: New consensus on developmentally supportive follow-up with color-coded risk stratification and validated screening tools; WHO 2022: 11 new recommendations for preterm/LBW care including home visits by trained health workers; AAP 2023: Primary care framework for neurodevelopmental monitoring of preterm infants
counselling: Continue breastfeeding as first choice, feed every 2-3 hours
Give all supplements regularly as prescribed - do not skip
Target weight gain is 15-20 g/kg/day
Report immediately if poor feeding or poor weight gain
Report immediately if lethargy or repeated vomiting
Report immediately if seizures or abnormal movements
Report immediately if breathing difficulty
Follow-up every 2 weeks initially, then monthly
Keep all immunization appointments on schedule (use chronological age)
Normal development is possible with proper follow-up
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if poor weight gain despite adequate feeding, developmental delay, suspected ROP (retinopathy of prematurity), or recurrent infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if failure to thrive, severe anemia, apnea episodes, suspected sepsis, or respiratory distress

investigations.name: ROP Screening
investigations.indication: Retinopathy of prematurity screening as per schedule

investigations.name: Weight monitoring
investigations.indication: Weekly weight check for growth assessment

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or poor weight gain

first_line_drugs.notes: If SpO2 <92%, nasal prongs/hood/HFNC, target SpO2 92-94%. HFNC 1-2 L/kg/min for moderate-severe distress
3 ml NEB q4-6h, preferred nebulization for bronchiolitis
Gentle suction before feeds, improves feeding and breathing

second_line_drugs.notes: NEB trial only if strong wheeze or atopy history. Not routine
3 ml NEB, optional in hospital setting
Mucolytic, limited evidence, can be used symptomatically

notes: Viral infection (commonly RSV), self-limiting (peak Day 3-5, recovery 10-14 days). Oxygen therapy if SpO2 <92%, target 92-94%. HFNC for moderate-severe distress (1-2 L/kg/min). Bronchodilators NOT routine. Steroids NOT routinely recommended. Antibiotics NOT indicated unless secondary bacterial infection. Maintain hydration 100-120 ml/kg/day. Continue breastfeeding if possible. NG feeds/IV fluids if feeding difficulty.
source: AAP 2014 (reaffirmed 2024), IAP 2024, WHO 2023, AIIMS Protocol
guideline_changes: AAP 2014/2024: No routine albuterol, epinephrine, hypertonic saline, or chest physiotherapy in outpatient bronchiolitis; supportive care only (oxygen + hydration); AAP 2024: Nirsevimab recommended for RSV prophylaxis in all infants <8 months entering first RSV season; IAP 2024: Aligned with AAP on no routine nebulization
counselling: This is a viral illness - antibiotics are not needed
Illness peaks at Day 3-5 and usually recovers in 10-14 days
Oxygen is the main treatment - avoid unnecessary medications
Continue breastfeeding and ensure adequate hydration
Report immediately if severe chest retractions
Report immediately if apnea (breathing pauses) episodes
Report immediately if SpO2 drops below 90%
Report immediately if poor feeding (<50% of usual)
Report immediately if lethargy or drowsiness
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if HFNC failure, recurrent wheezing episodes, or atypical course suggesting alternative diagnosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, moderate-severe respiratory distress, apnea episodes, feeding difficulty, age <3 months, or dehydration. ICU if HFNC failure, persistent hypoxia, apnea, or exhaustion

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Severe or prolonged fever to rule out bacterial co-infection

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if atypical course or suspected complication

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or taking less than half of usual feeds

first_line_drugs.notes: NEB: <10 kg 0.63 mg, >=10 kg 1.25 mg, every 20 min x3 doses (acute), then q6-8h. Syrup: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/dose TDS PO
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 3-5 days, for moderate-severe episodes. Reduces airway inflammation and prevents worsening
Age-appropriate dose PO BD, if allergic component present
Mucolytic/expectorant, 2.5 ml TDS PO, use selectively - not mandatory in all cases

notes: Nebulization is first-line. Steroids reduce relapse. Transition to inhaler therapy (MDI + Spacer) for long-term: Salbutamol inhaler as reliever, Budesonide/Fluticasone as controller. Inhaler > Syrup for long-term management. Avoid triggers (dust, smoke, cold). Avoid overuse of cough syrups. Antibiotics not required unless infection suspected.
source: GINA 2024/2025, IAP Asthma Guidelines 2024
guideline_changes: GINA 2025: New diagnostic criteria for asthma in children <5 years (3 required: recurrent wheezing, no alternative cause, response to asthma meds); GINA 2024: Updated stepwise approach emphasizing early ICS; IAP 2024: Inhaler therapy preferred over oral medications for long-term control
counselling: Nebulization is the most important treatment - use as directed
Complete the full course of oral steroid (3-5 days), do not stop early
Avoid triggers: dust, smoke, cold exposure, strong smells
Steam inhalation and adequate hydration help recovery
Transition to inhaler therapy (MDI + Spacer) for long-term control
Report immediately if severe breathing difficulty or chest retractions
Report immediately if unable to speak or feed
Report immediately if SpO2 drops below 92%
Report immediately if no response to nebulization
Follow up after 3-5 days for reassessment and long-term plan
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if no improvement in 48 hours, recurrent severe episodes (>3/year), need for step-up beyond Step 2 GINA, or suspected alternative diagnosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe respiratory distress, SpO2 <92%, no response to initial nebulization, inability to feed, or toxic appearance

investigations.name: Pulse oximetry
investigations.indication: All acute asthma episodes - assess oxygenation

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If suspected complication (pneumothorax, pneumonia) or first episode

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Severe breathing difficulty or unable to speak
No response to nebulization or inhaler

first_line_drugs.notes: MDI + Spacer + Mask, 100 mcg/puff, 2 puffs QID (5-6 breaths per puff via spacer). Reliever - continue till symptoms controlled, stop 5 days after complete relief
100-200 mcg/puff MDI, 1 puff BD (q12h) via spacer. Controller - minimum 3 months, then step-down. Do NOT stop abruptly
NEB: <10 kg 0.63 mg, >=10 kg 1.25 mg, every 20 min x3 doses for acute relief, then q6-8h
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 3-5 days, only for moderate episodes
Age-appropriate dose PO BD, if allergic component

notes: Inhaler therapy is the GOLD STANDARD - faster action, fewer side effects than oral drugs. Spacer + Mask mandatory for proper drug delivery in infants and young children. GINA Stepwise: Step 1 SABA PRN, Step 2 low-dose ICS, Step 3 ICS+LABA, Step 4 specialist referral. ICS is preventive (not immediate relief). SABA is rescue drug. Early inhaler use reduces hospital admissions.
source: GINA 2024/2025, IAP Asthma Guidelines 2024
guideline_changes: GINA 2025: New diagnostic criteria for asthma in children <5 years; expanded role of T2 biomarkers (blood eosinophils, FeNO) in assessment; GINA 2024: MDI+Spacer remains preferred delivery for all ages; Step-down only after 3 months symptom-free on low-dose ICS
counselling: Inhaler is always better than syrup - faster, safer, fewer side effects
Use spacer with mask for proper drug delivery
Inhaler technique: shake, attach spacer, place mask tightly, press 1 puff, allow 5-6 breaths, repeat if needed
Controller inhaler (Budesonide) must be taken daily for minimum 3 months - do NOT stop suddenly
Reliever inhaler (Salbutamol) is for rescue use during symptoms only
Report immediately if severe chest retractions or SpO2 <92%
Report immediately if no response to inhaler
Report immediately if feeding difficulty
Follow up in 3-5 days, then regular follow-up for step-up/step-down assessment
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if requiring Step 3 or above, frequent exacerbations despite ICS, or diagnostic uncertainty
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe respiratory distress, SpO2 <92%, no response to inhaler therapy, or inability to feed

investigations.name: Peak expiratory flow rate
investigations.indication: Children >5 years for severity and treatment response monitoring

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If first episode or suspected complication

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Feeding difficulty or unable to eat

first_line_drugs.notes: NEB: <10 kg 0.63 mg, >=10 kg 1.25-2.5 mg, every 20 min x3 doses (first hour), then q1-2h, taper to q4-6h
1-2 mg/kg/dose IV STAT, then q6-8h. Mandatory early steroid. Reduces airway inflammation and prevents progression
Nasal cannula/mask, 2-6 L/min, target SpO2 >=94%
25-50 mg/kg IV in 30-50 ml NS over 20-30 min, for severe cases. Bronchodilator effect, reduces ICU need
250 mcg NEB q6h, add-on bronchodilator
0.01 mg/kg SC (1:1000), repeat every 15-20 min x2-3 doses. For severe bronchospasm / impending respiratory failure only

notes: Golden Hour: Oxygen + aggressive nebulization + systemic steroids. Monitoring: SpO2 continuous, RR/HR/work of breathing hourly, ABG if severe. Fluids 100 ml/kg/day, avoid overload. Antibiotics NOT routine - only if fever + infection suspected. Escalation: HFNC if moderate distress, NIV if worsening, mechanical ventilation as last resort. Step-down: transition to MDI+Spacer (Salbutamol reliever + Budesonide controller) and oral steroids before discharge.
source: GINA 2024/2025, IAP Guidelines 2024, AIIMS Protocol, NABH HCO 6th Edition
guideline_changes: GINA 2025: IV isotonic MgSO4 may be considered in children >=2 years with severe exacerbations; GINA 2024: Early systemic steroids within first hour remain critical; Ipratropium bromide add-on for severe episodes; Emphasis on step-down to MDI+Spacer before discharge with written action plan
counselling: Child needs indoor admission for intensive monitoring and treatment
Oxygen and nebulization are the main treatment
Steroids are essential and will be given early to reduce inflammation
Report any worsening immediately: increased breathing difficulty, drowsiness, or bluish discoloration
After discharge, inhaler therapy (Salbutamol + Budesonide) will be started for long-term control
Learn proper inhaler technique before discharge
Follow up after 3-5 days of discharge
Avoid triggers: dust, smoke, cold exposure, strong smells
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU if SpO2 <90% despite oxygen, silent chest, rising CO2 on ABG, exhaustion/drowsiness, or need for ventilatory support (HFNC/NIV/mechanical ventilation)
hospitalisation_criteria: Already indoor protocol. ICU transfer if: SpO2 <90% despite oxygen, silent chest, rising CO2 on ABG, exhaustion or drowsiness, HFNC failure

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumothorax, pneumonia, or other complications

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Baseline and to rule out infection

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Drop in oxygen saturation below 90% despite oxygen

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/dose PO 6-8 hourly SOS for fever >100°F
As per weight, TDS PO for 3-5 days, add-on for bodyache/high fever

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO for 5-7 days, only if persistent fever >3 days or lab evidence of bacterial infection

notes: Symptomatic treatment first. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless persistent fever >3 days, toxic child, raised CRP/Procalcitonin, or localizing signs. Supportive: adequate fluids (ORS, water, coconut water), light diet, tepid sponging, rest.
source: IAP Fever Guidelines, AIIMS Febrile Child Protocol, NICE Fever in Under 5s
guideline_changes: IAP 2024: Procalcitonin recommended as key biomarker to differentiate bacterial vs viral fever; emphasis on avoiding empirical antibiotics in first 72 hours unless toxic appearance or positive cultures; AIIMS Protocol: Structured fever workup with CBC, CRP, urine R/M, blood culture for fever >3 days; NICE 2024: Age-stratified approach - infants <3 months with fever require urgent evaluation and empiric antibiotics
counselling: Give paracetamol only when fever is above 100°F, do not give routinely
Offer frequent fluids - ORS, water, coconut water - to prevent dehydration
Use tepid sponging with lukewarm water for high fever, do not use cold water
If fever persists beyond 2-3 days, bring child for review with reports
Watch for warning signs: rash, bleeding, vomiting, altered sensorium, breathing difficulty - come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer if fever persists >5 days without identifiable focus, suspected enteric fever or tropical infection requiring specialist evaluation, or immunocompromised child
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if toxic appearance, severe dehydration, altered sensorium, bleeding manifestations, persistent high-grade fever with hemodynamic instability, or age <3 months with fever

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Severe or prolonged fever to identify organism

investigations.name: Malaria antigen / Dengue NS1 + IgM
investigations.indication: If endemic area or clinical suspicion

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent fever for more than 3 days
Rash or any bleeding from skin or gums

first_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO for 5-7 days
10-15 mg/kg/dose PO 6-8 hourly SOS for fever/pain
5-10 mg/kg/dose PO 6-8 hourly SOS, for fever and throat pain
If child >5 years, adequate hydration, soft diet (khichdi, soups). Avoid cold items

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose BD PO for 7-10 days, reserve drug for severe/non-responding/resistant cases only

notes: Start antibiotic if high-grade fever with follicles or toxic child. Supportive: warm saline gargles (if child >5 years), adequate hydration, soft diet. Avoid cold items.
source: IAP Guidelines, IDSA Pharyngitis Guidelines 2012/2024 update, AHA Rheumatic Fever Prevention
guideline_changes: IDSA 2024: Continued emphasis on rapid antigen detection test (RADT) or culture before starting antibiotics for pharyngitis; Centor/McIsaac scoring to guide testing; Penicillin V or amoxicillin remain first-line for confirmed GAS; IAP 2024: Antibiotic stewardship - avoid empirical antibiotics for viral tonsillitis; reserve cephalosporins for penicillin-allergic patients
counselling: Complete full course of antibiotic even if child feels better in 2-3 days
Give warm fluids and soft diet (khichdi, soups) to ease swallowing
Warm saline gargles help if child is older than 5 years
Paracetamol/ibuprofen will control fever and throat pain
Return if fever persists beyond 3 days, difficulty swallowing saliva, neck swelling, or breathing difficulty
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent tonsillitis (>7 episodes/year or >5/year for 2 years), suspected peritonsillar abscess, or airway compromise
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if unable to swallow, severe dehydration, peritonsillar abscess, toxic appearance, or breathing difficulty
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent fever for more than 3 days

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 puff each nostril BD, continue at least 4-6 weeks, regular use not SOS
6-12 yrs: 60-120 mg OD PO; >12 yrs: 120-180 mg OD PO, for 7-14 days or longer
2-3 drops each nostril 2-3 times/day, clears allergens and improves drug delivery
1 puff each nostril BD, for moderate-severe or persistent rhinitis, faster symptom relief

notes: ARIA classification: Intermittent (<4 days/week) vs Persistent (>4 days/week); Mild vs Moderate-Severe. Intranasal steroid is gold standard. Allergen avoidance is essential: dust, smoke, perfumes, cold exposure, pets, house dust mites.
source: ARIA-EAACI Guidelines 2024-2025, IAP Allergy Protocol, GINA
guideline_changes: ARIA-EAACI 2024-2025: Combined intranasal antihistamine + corticosteroid (INAH+INCS) recommended over monotherapy for moderate-severe AR; INCS preferred over INAH alone; digital shared decision-making algorithms introduced; pediatric considerations updated for preschool and school-age children; emphasis on step-up/step-down approach based on symptom control
counselling: Use nasal spray regularly every day, not just when symptoms are bad
Avoid dust, smoke, perfumes, and cold exposure - these are common triggers
Saline nasal drops before steroid spray improve drug delivery
Improvement takes 3-5 days, full control in 2-3 weeks - do not stop early
Keep home dust-free, use cotton bedding, and avoid pets if allergic
referral_criteria: Refer to allergist/ENT if poor response to 4-6 weeks of treatment, severe persistent symptoms, associated asthma requiring step-up therapy, or need for allergy testing
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed. Consider if severe associated asthma exacerbation or complications like sinusitis requiring IV antibiotics

investigations.name: CBC with absolute eosinophil count
investigations.indication: Allergy indicator, elevated eosinophils suggest atopy

investigations.name: Allergy skin prick test / specific IgE
investigations.indication: Identify specific triggers in recurrent/severe cases

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Severe nasal blockage causing difficulty breathing
Disturbed sleep or mouth breathing at night
Symptoms not improving despite regular medication

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply liberally 2-3 times daily, long-term use even after improvement, cornerstone of therapy
Apply thin layer once daily for 5-7 days acute flare, then taper to 2 times/week maintenance, avoid prolonged use on face
Apply once daily at night for scalp/extensive lesions, 1-2 weeks then taper
Natural moisturization, coconut oil in summer, aloe vera-based lotion in winter, safe and soothing
Age-appropriate dose PO for 5-7 days, for pruritus and sleep disturbance
Age-appropriate dose PO for 5-7 days, alternative antihistamine for itching

notes: Treatment principles: restore skin barrier, reduce inflammation, control itching, prevent relapse. Moisturizer is cornerstone. Steroid for flare only - avoid overuse. Lukewarm water bath, mild soap-free cleanser, apply moisturizer within 3 minutes after bath. Avoid harsh soaps, woolen clothes, dust/allergens.
source: AAP Atopic Dermatitis Clinical Report 2025, AAD Guidelines 2024, AAAAI/ACAAI Joint Task Force 2023, NICE
guideline_changes: AAP 2025: Updated clinical report on skin-directed management - daily bathing with lukewarm water and gentle cleanser, moisturizer within minutes of bath; proactive intermittent topical anti-inflammatory maintenance to prevent flares; AAD 2024: Updated treatment guidelines with expanded role of topical calcineurin inhibitors and JAK inhibitors for moderate-severe disease; AAAAI 2023: Dupilumab recommended for moderate-severe AD in children >=6 months
counselling: Apply moisturizer generously 2-3 times daily - this is the most important treatment
Apply moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing to lock in moisture
Use steroid cream only during flare-ups as directed, do not use continuously
Use lukewarm water for bath and mild soap-free cleanser only
Avoid woolen clothes, harsh soaps, dust, and suspected food allergens
This is a chronic condition with relapses - regular skin care prevents flare-ups
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if no improvement with topical steroids after 2 weeks, severe widespread disease, suspected secondary infection requiring systemic treatment, or need for immunomodulators (tacrolimus/pimecrolimus)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if erythroderma, severe secondary bacterial infection (oozing, fever), eczema herpeticum, or severe disease not responding to outpatient management

investigations.name: CBC with absolute eosinophil count
investigations.indication: Allergy workup, elevated eosinophils suggest atopy

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Oozing or pus from skin lesions suggesting infection
Severe redness or swelling of affected skin
No response to treatment after one week

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 ml/kg/day PO div BD, after meals or at bedtime, first-line stool softener
Apply locally before passing stool, 2-3 times/day for pain relief
5-10 ml PO at bedtime for 5-7 days, stool bulking and smooth passage
Warm water with light pink KMnO4 solution, 10-15 minutes 2-3 times/day, promotes healing and reduces pain
SOS for acute constipation, short-term use only
1 teaspoon (3-5 g) in 1 glass water at bedtime OD for 5-7 days, fiber supplement for stool consistency

notes: Treatment principles: soften stool, relieve pain, heal fissure, prevent recurrence. Sitz bath with warm water + potassium permanganate (light pink solution) for 10-15 min 2-3 times/day is essential. High fiber diet: papaya, banana, vegetables. Adequate water intake. Avoid junk food and excess milk.
source: IAP Pediatric Surgery Guidelines, ASCRS Anal Fissure Guidelines 2023, ESPGHAN Constipation Guidelines
guideline_changes: ASCRS 2023: Topical calcium channel blockers (diltiazem) recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy for chronic fissures with better side-effect profile than GTN; conservative management (stool softeners, fiber, sitz baths) remains first-choice for acute fissures in children; IAP Pediatric Surgery 2024: PEG-based laxatives preferred over lactulose for chronic constipation-associated fissures
counselling: Give lactulose regularly to keep stool soft - this is the most important treatment
Sitz bath in warm water for 10-15 minutes 2-3 times daily helps healing
Apply lignocaine gel before passing stool to reduce pain
Increase fiber in diet - papaya, banana, green vegetables, adequate water
Avoid junk food and excess milk which worsen constipation
Return if persistent bleeding, severe pain not relieved, or no improvement in 5-7 days
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if chronic fissure not healing after 4-6 weeks of conservative treatment, recurrent fissures, or suspected underlying condition (Crohn disease, anorectal anomaly)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if significant rectal bleeding, suspected perianal abscess, or need for examination under anesthesia

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Rule out occult blood, parasites, or infection if recurrent

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Assess for anemia if significant or recurrent bleeding

investigations.name: Stool occult blood test
investigations.indication: Confirm GI bleeding if history of blood in stool

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent or increasing bleeding during stools
Severe pain not relieved by medication
No improvement in symptoms after 5 to 7 days

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 ml/kg after each loose stool, frequent small sips, life-saving
8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO for 5-7 days
<6 months: 10 mg/day; >=6 months: 20 mg/day PO for 14 days, reduces severity and recurrence
10 mg/kg/dose TDS PO for 5 days, for suspected amoebic or mixed infection
Once or twice daily PO for 5 days, probiotic to restore gut flora and reduce diarrhea duration
Age/weight appropriate dose TDS PO, short-term, for abdominal pain/cramps

second_line_drugs.notes: Consider for persistent diarrhea or resistant infection, not routine first-line

notes: ORS + Zinc = cornerstone of therapy. Continue normal feeding. Antibiotic indicated for bloody dysentery. Preferred diet: curd, buttermilk, khichdi, rice, banana, coconut water. Avoid junk food and sugary drinks.
source: IAP Diarrhea Guidelines, WHO Diarrheal Disease Management, CDC Shigella Treatment Guidelines 2024, IDSA Infectious Diarrhea Guidelines
guideline_changes: WHO 2024: Rising antimicrobial resistance in Shigella globally - azithromycin resistance increased significantly; CDC 2023: Alert on extensively drug-resistant (XDR) Shigella strains; ceftriaxone or azithromycin recommended where ciprofloxacin resistance is high; stool culture and sensitivity recommended for non-responding cases; IAP 2024: ORS + Zinc remains cornerstone; cefixime preferred empiric oral antibiotic for pediatric bloody dysentery in India
counselling: ORS is the most important treatment - give after every loose stool
Continue normal feeding, do not stop food - give curd, khichdi, rice, banana
Give zinc syrup daily for 14 days even after diarrhea stops
Complete full course of antibiotic as prescribed
Wash hands before feeding and after toilet to prevent spread
Come immediately if blood in stool increases, persistent vomiting, no urine, child becomes lethargic or severely dehydrated
referral_criteria: Refer if no improvement in 48 hours on antibiotics, suspected hemolytic uremic syndrome, recurrent dysentery, or suspected resistant organism
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, unable to drink or retain ORS, bloody stool with hemodynamic instability, persistent vomiting, lethargy/altered sensorium, or age <2 months

investigations.name: Stool culture and sensitivity
investigations.indication: Severe or non-responding cases to identify organism

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent vomiting or unable to drink
No urine output for more than 6 hours
Signs of severe dehydration such as sunken eyes or dry mouth

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 ml/kg after each loose stool, small frequent sips PO
<6 months: 10 mg/day, >=6 months: 20 mg/day PO x 14 days
0.15 mg/kg/dose BD-TDS PO, short duration 2-3 days, if vomiting
OD-BD PO x 5 days, probiotic to restore gut flora
Age-appropriate dose TDS PO, short-term, only if significant abdominal pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO x 5 days, only if dysentery/high fever/toxic child
10 mg/kg/dose TDS PO x 5 days, only if suspected protozoal/mixed infection

notes: ORS + Zinc = gold standard. Continue feeding. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics. Antibiotics only for dysentery, high fever, or toxic child. Assess dehydration status at each visit.
source: IAP 2024, WHO Diarrhoea Management Guidelines
guideline_changes: WHO 2024: conditional recommendation for reduced zinc dose (5 mg/day) in children up to 10 yr to lower vomiting risk while preserving efficacy; probiotics not recommended due to low-certainty evidence; ondansetron endorsed for single-dose anti-emetic use in ED/OPD before ORS.
counselling: Give ORS after every loose stool - it is life-saving and prevents dehydration
Continue breastfeeding and normal diet - do not stop food
Give Zinc for full 14 days even if diarrhoea stops
Preferred foods: curd, buttermilk, khichdi, rice, banana, coconut water
Avoid junk food, carbonated drinks, and outside food
If child has persistent vomiting, no urine, lethargy, blood in stool, or severe dehydration - come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if persistent diarrhoea >14 days, recurrent episodes, suspected malabsorption, or failure to thrive
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, persistent vomiting with inability to take ORS, lethargy/altered sensorium, blood in stool with toxic appearance, age <2 months, or failed OPD rehydration

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Suspected bacterial infection or toxic child

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent vomiting or unable to drink
No urine output for more than 6 hours
Signs of severe dehydration such as sunken eyes or dry mouth

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose BD-TDS PO, short duration 2-3 days, for vomiting control
1 mg/kg/day OD PO before meals x 7-10 days, first-line acid suppression
Small frequent sips PO, especially if vomiting or mild diarrhoea
1 mg/kg/day OD PO before meals x 7-10 days, alternative PPI
OD PO x 3-5 days, optional probiotic for gut recovery
Age-appropriate dose TDS PO, short-term, only if significant abdominal pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-4 mg/kg/dose BD PO x 7-10 days, if PPI not available/tolerated

notes: Ondansetron for vomiting control. PPI (Omeprazole) is first-line acid suppression, preferred over H2 blockers. Maintain hydration. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics. Small frequent meals.
source: IAP 2024, Pediatric GI Guidelines
guideline_changes: ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN 2024: updated H. pylori guidelines with bismuth-based quadruple therapy for resistant strains; PPIs remain first-line acid suppression in children; PPI deprescription emphasized after 4-8 wk courses; ACG 2024 highlights rising clarithromycin resistance.
counselling: Give small frequent meals instead of large meals
Preferred foods: curd, buttermilk, khichdi, rice, banana, coconut water
Avoid spicy food, fried food, and junk food
Keep child well hydrated with ORS and fluids
If persistent vomiting, blood in vomit, severe abdominal pain, or dehydration - come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if persistent vomiting >5 days despite treatment, recurrent episodes, suspected H. pylori, or upper GI bleeding
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if persistent vomiting with dehydration, hematemesis, severe abdominal pain with guarding, or inability to tolerate oral intake

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Associated loose stools or suspected infection

investigations.name: Liver function test
investigations.indication: Persistent vomiting to rule out hepatic cause

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent vomiting or blood in vomit
Severe abdominal pain not relieved by medication
Signs of dehydration such as dry mouth or no urine

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg/day IV OD or BD, dilute in 100 ml NS over 30-60 min, x 7-10 days
10-15 mg/kg/dose IV 6 hourly for fever control
0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose IV 8 hourly for vomiting
Maintenance rate per Holliday-Segar formula (~60-80 ml/hr adjust clinically), maintain hydration and correct electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day IV over 1-2 hours x 5-7 days, add-on for moderate-severe/resistant cases
8 mg/kg/day div BD PO, step-down oral antibiotic after afebrile 48-72 hours, complete 10-14 days total

notes: Indoor protocol. Ceftriaxone is first-line IV drug. IV fluids (DNS/NS) as per Holliday-Segar maintenance. Monitor temperature 4-hourly, pulse/BP 4-hourly, strict I/O charting, daily sensorium and abdominal exam. Step-down to oral antibiotics when afebrile 48-72 hrs. Complete 10-14 days total therapy. Watch for intestinal perforation, GI bleeding, encephalopathy, shock.
source: IAP 2024, WHO Typhoid Guidelines, AIIMS Protocol
guideline_changes: WHO/CDC 2024: azithromycin elevated as co-first-line with ceftriaxone due to rising XDR S. Typhi (especially South Asia); meropenem recommended for XDR strains; blood culture before antibiotics strongly emphasized; TCV single-dose conjugate vaccine now in routine immunisation in endemic countries.
counselling: Typhoid requires a full course of antibiotics - do not stop early even if fever resolves
Expected hospital stay is 3-5 days until fever-free
Give soft easily digestible diet: khichdi, curd, buttermilk, banana, coconut water
Avoid spicy and oily food during recovery
Complete the full course of oral antibiotics after discharge
Follow-up blood tests may be needed to confirm cure
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric infectious disease specialist if no defervescence after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics, suspected MDR typhoid, or complications (perforation, encephalopathy, myocarditis)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all moderate-severe enteric fever. Also admit if: persistent high fever, toxic appearance, vomiting with inability to take oral antibiotics, suspected complications, age <2 years, or immunocompromised child

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis, before starting antibiotics

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Abdominal distension or severe abdominal pain
Signs of GI bleeding such as blood in stool or black stools
Persistent high fever despite treatment for more than 5 days

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15-0.6 mg/kg single dose PO/IM/IV, max 16 mg; gold standard, reduces airway edema
0.5 ml/kg of 1:1000 solution (max 5 ml) nebulised, repeat after 20-30 min if needed; for moderate-severe cases with stridor at rest
If SpO2 < 92%, via nasal prongs or mask
Age/weight appropriate dose nebulised or PO, optional bronchial/cough support, not primary treatment for croup
Age-appropriate dose PO, only if allergic component present

notes: Dexamethasone single dose is the single most effective treatment. Adrenaline nebulisation for emergency relief in moderate-severe cases - observe 2-4 hours after dose for recurrence. Keep child calm (crying worsens stridor). Humidified air, adequate fluids. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics. Severity: Mild = barking cough no stridor at rest; Moderate = stridor at rest with mild retractions; Severe = marked distress with hypoxia.
source: IAP 2024, AAP Croup Guidelines, WHO
guideline_changes: 2024 cohort data: single-dose dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) remains gold standard; repeat dosing in hospitalised children showed longer LOS without benefit on 30-day ED return; 0.15 mg/kg may suffice for mild croup; nebulised epinephrine observation period confirmed at 2-4 hr.
counselling: Croup causes a barking cough and noisy breathing - keep child calm as crying worsens symptoms
Use humidified air or steam in the room
Give adequate fluids and small frequent meals
Improvement expected within 24 hours after steroid dose
Full recovery usually in 3-5 days
If stridor persists at rest, child has severe breathing difficulty, SpO2 drops, or child becomes lethargic - come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist or ENT if recurrent croup (>2 episodes), atypical presentation, suspected foreign body, or subglottic stenosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if stridor at rest persists after dexamethasone and adrenaline, severe retractions, SpO2 <92%, lethargy/exhaustion, poor oral intake, or need for repeated adrenaline nebulisation

investigations.name: Neck X-ray AP view
investigations.indication: Steeple sign if diagnosis uncertain or atypical

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Stridor at rest that does not improve
Poor oral intake or refusal to drink

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/dose 6-8 hourly PO SOS, pain relief is first priority
5-10 mg/kg/dose 6-8 hourly PO SOS, for pain and fever
40-45 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin component) div BD PO x 5-7 days, drug of choice
Age-appropriate dose TDS PO, short duration, nasal decongestant to improve Eustachian tube function

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day div BD PO x 5-7 days, use if penicillin allergy or poor response
2-3 drops TDS in affected ear, only if external ear involvement or discharge; avoid if tympanic membrane perforation

notes: Pain control is the priority. Amox-Clav is first-line antibiotic. Avoid dual antibiotic therapy (Amox-Clav + Cefpodoxime simultaneously not recommended). Ear drops only if external ear involvement or discharge (avoid if TM perforation unless appropriate drops). Non-pharmacological: warm compress over ear, adequate hydration, keep ear dry.
source: IAP 2024, AAP AOM Guidelines
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 reaffirms watchful waiting for mild unilateral AOM in children >=2 yr; high-dose amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg/day) remains first-line; amoxicillin-clavulanate for treatment failure or beta-lactamase producers; Italian intersociety 2024 consensus aligns with AAP on shortened 5-day courses for uncomplicated AOM in children >=2 yr.
counselling: Ear pain usually improves in 24-48 hours with treatment
Complete the full course of antibiotics even if pain resolves
Apply warm compress over the affected ear for pain relief
Keep the ear dry - avoid water entering the ear during bathing
If pain persists beyond 48 hours, high fever continues, ear discharge appears, or swelling behind the ear develops - return to hospital immediately
Follow up in 2-3 days to assess response to treatment
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT specialist if no improvement after 48-72 hours on antibiotics, recurrent AOM (>3 episodes in 6 months or >4 in 1 year), suspected mastoiditis, hearing difficulty, or chronic ear discharge
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected mastoiditis (post-auricular swelling/tenderness), intracranial complications (meningitis, brain abscess), toxic appearance, age <2 months with fever, or failed outpatient therapy with systemic signs
warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent ear pain beyond 48 hours despite treatment
High fever not responding to medication
Ear discharge or pus from the ear
Swelling behind the ear suggesting mastoiditis

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day div BD PO, hepatoprotective/cholestasis support
3-5 ml BD PO, age-appropriate dose, appetite improvement and hepatoprotective support
1-2 ml/kg/day PO, adjust to maintain 2 soft stools/day, prevents encephalopathy
IV infusion per paracetamol-poisoning protocol dosing, for severe hepatitis/rising liver enzymes in admitted cases
ORS and oral fluids; IV fluids if vomiting prevents oral intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg IM/IV x 3 days, if INR prolonged/coagulation deranged

notes: Mostly supportive treatment. No routine antivirals needed for HAV/HEV. Diet + hydration = main treatment. High carbohydrate, low fat diet with frequent small meals. Monitor INR carefully. ORS/fluids orally; IV fluids if vomiting. NAC infusion in all admitted cases with severe hepatitis. Recovery in 2-4 weeks, full normalization in 4-6 weeks.
source: IAP 2024, WHO Hepatitis Guidelines, AIIMS Protocol
guideline_changes: INASL/AASLD 2024: IV NAC recommended in all non-acetaminophen acute liver failure (150 mg/kg loading then 50+100 mg/kg maintenance); early NAC initiation critical for better outcomes; no routine antivirals for HAV/HEV; EASL 2025 updated chronic HBV guidelines but acute management remains supportive.
counselling: Hepatitis is a liver infection - mostly recovers on its own with rest and diet
Give high carbohydrate, low fat diet: khichdi, banana, coconut water, curd, buttermilk
Give small frequent meals instead of large meals
Avoid fatty and fried food completely during recovery
Keep child well hydrated with ORS and fluids
Repeat liver function tests after 5-7 days as advised
If persistent vomiting, altered consciousness, bleeding tendency, increasing jaundice, or dark urine worsens - come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hepatologist if INR >1.5, signs of hepatic encephalopathy, fulminant hepatic failure, no improvement after 2 weeks, or suspected chronic hepatitis (HBV/HCV)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if persistent vomiting with dehydration, altered sensorium, bleeding tendency (INR >1.5), increasing jaundice with rising bilirubin, suspected fulminant hepatic failure, or age <1 year

investigations.name: Hepatitis A IgM / Hepatitis E IgM
Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Monitoring with IV fluids or persistent vomiting

investigations.name: Serum ammonia
investigations.indication: If altered sensorium - rule out encephalopathy

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Persistent vomiting or inability to eat
Bleeding tendency such as nose bleed or blood in vomit

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 ml/kg IV bolus over 30-60 min for initial rehydration; maintenance per Holliday-Segar formula
0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose IV q8h for vomiting
10-15 mg/kg/dose IV q6h for fever
1 sachet BD PO, reduces duration of diarrhea
Start early when tolerated, small frequent sips
20 mg OD PO for 14 days (<6 months: 10 mg OD), start with oral transition

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg/day IV OD/BD, only if dysentery/sepsis/toxic child
15 mg/kg/day IV OD, add-on if severe sepsis suspected
7.5-10 mg/kg/dose IV TDS, only if amoebic dysentery suspected

notes: IV fluids are the cornerstone. Maintenance fluid per Holliday-Segar: 100 ml/kg first 10 kg + 50 ml/kg next 10 kg. Avoid routine triple antibiotics unless clearly indicated. Early transition to ORS. Continue feeding as early as possible (curd, khichdi, banana, coconut water).
source: IAP 2024 Diarrhea Guidelines, WHO Diarrheal Disease Management, NASPGHAN 2024 AGE Clinical Pathway
guideline_changes: NASPGHAN 2024 AGE update: Lactobacillus not routinely recommended (two large RCTs showed no benefit); ondansetron preferred antiemetic for children >=6 months; early oral rehydration emphasized over IV where possible; Saccharomyces boulardii retains moderate evidence for reducing diarrhea duration.
counselling: Continue breastfeeding and complementary foods during illness
Give ORS sip by sip after every loose stool
Continue zinc for full 14 days even after diarrhea stops
Offer curd, khichdi, banana, coconut water as tolerated
Return immediately if child becomes lethargic, stops urinating, or has blood in stool
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if persistent diarrhea >14 days, recurrent severe episodes, suspected malabsorption, or failure to thrive
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, unable to tolerate oral fluids, persistent vomiting, toxic appearance, age <2 months, decreased urine output, or electrolyte imbalance

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: If severe dehydration or prolonged diarrhea

investigations.name: Renal function tests
investigations.indication: Rule out acute kidney injury in severe dehydration

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Signs of severe dehydration such as sunken eyes or no tears
Electrolyte imbalance symptoms such as muscle cramps or weakness
No urine output for more than 6 hours
Signs of shock such as cold hands or weak pulse

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 drops in each nostril 2-3 times daily, keeps mucosa moist
2-3 drops each nostril TDS for mucosal hydration
Age-appropriate dose OD PO for 2-4 weeks, improves capillary strength
Sit child upright, tilt head slightly forward, pinch soft part of nose for 10 minutes, apply cold compress

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply inside anterior nares BD for 5 days, if crusting/infection present
Apply inside anterior nares BD for 5 days, alternative to mupirocin if crusting/infection

notes: Most cases are benign anterior bleeds from Kiesselbach plexus due to nose picking or dry mucosa. Moisturization is the cornerstone. Teach first aid: sit upright, tilt head forward, pinch soft nose for 10 min, cold compress. Keep nails short. Avoid nose picking, forceful blowing, dry air.
source: AAO-HNS Clinical Practice Guideline: Nosebleed (2020, AAP-endorsed), IAP ENT Guidelines
guideline_changes: AAO-HNS 2020 CPG (AAP-endorsed): sustained pressure for minimum 5 minutes on lower third of nose; topical tranexamic acid now recommended as superior to other agents for stopping bleeding in first 10 minutes; oxymetazoline preferred topical vasoconstrictor. No major 2024-2025 pediatric-specific updates.
counselling: Apply nasal oil drops regularly to keep the nose moist
Teach first aid: sit upright, tilt head forward, pinch soft part of nose for 10 minutes
Keep child's nails short to prevent nose picking
Avoid forceful nose blowing and dry air exposure
Return if bleeding lasts >15-20 minutes, is recurrent, or bleeding from other sites
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT specialist if recurrent frequent episodes (>1/week), bleeding from posterior nose, suspected bleeding disorder, or epistaxis not controlled by local measures
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if active uncontrollable bleeding >20 minutes despite compression, hemodynamic instability, significant blood loss with pallor/weakness, or suspected posterior epistaxis requiring nasal packing

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Recurrent or severe epistaxis to check platelets and anemia

investigations.name: PT/INR
investigations.indication: Recurrent epistaxis to rule out coagulation disorder

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Bleeding lasting more than 15 to 20 minutes despite pressure
Bleeding from other sites such as gums or skin bruises
Pallor or weakness suggesting significant blood loss

first_line_drugs.notes: Explain to parents: common condition, improves with age, not child's fault; mandatory first step
Fluid management (avoid excess after 6-7 PM), timed voiding before bed, bladder training (hold urine for increasing intervals), positive reinforcement with bladder diary
Most effective long-term therapy, use for 3-4 months if available
0.2 mg (200 mcg) PO at bedtime, can increase to 0.4 mg if needed; duration 4-12 weeks then taper; restrict fluids after dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.9-1.5 mg/kg/dose PO at bedtime (max 50 mg for <12y, 75 mg for >=12y); use only if desmopressin fails; monitor for cardiac side effects; avoid in overdose-risk families
0.2-0.3 mg/kg/dose BD-TDS PO (max 5 mg TDS); useful when overactive bladder component present; may combine with desmopressin for refractory cases
1-2 mg PO at bedtime; alternative anticholinergic if oxybutynin not tolerated; fewer side effects than oxybutynin

notes: Behavioral therapy is first-line: fluid restriction after 6-7 PM, timed voiding before bed, wake child once at night, bladder training with increasing daytime holding intervals, positive reinforcement with reward calendar. Enuresis alarm is the most effective long-term therapy (use 3-4 months). Medication only if moderate-severe or significant social impact. Monitor for hyponatremia on desmopressin. Never punish the child.
source: ICCS 2020 Standardization Document, IAP Enuresis Protocol, J Pediatr Urol 2024
guideline_changes: ICCS 2020 updated standardization: active therapy from age 6 years; enuresis alarm remains most effective long-term treatment (3-4 months); combination desmopressin + imipramine shown more effective than desmopressin + oxybutynin for refractory cases (2024 study). No major 2024-2025 ICCS guideline revision.
counselling: Bedwetting is common and not the child's fault - never punish
Restrict fluids after 6-7 PM, encourage more fluids during daytime
Child should pass urine before bedtime every night
Reward dry nights with a star chart - positive reinforcement works
Most children outgrow bedwetting naturally; improvement expected in 4-8 weeks
If on desmopressin, restrict fluids after taking the dose to avoid low sodium
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist if daytime incontinence, weak urinary stream, recurrent UTI, secondary enuresis with red flags, or no response to 3 months of treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not typically required for primary enuresis. Admit only if severe hyponatremia on desmopressin or if secondary enuresis requires workup for underlying neurological/renal cause

investigations.name: Urine routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Rule out UTI in all cases

investigations.name: Ultrasound KUB
investigations.indication: Suspected structural abnormality or secondary enuresis

investigations.name: Blood sugar
investigations.indication: Rule out diabetes if polyuria or secondary enuresis

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Daytime incontinence in addition to bedwetting
Weak urine stream or straining to urinate
Secondary enuresis after being dry for more than 6 months

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 ml (10 drops) OD PO, up to 1 year of age
1-2 mg/kg/day elemental iron OD PO, start from 6 weeks-3 months depending on birth weight, continue up to 1 year
400 IU OD PO for at least 1 year, essential for bone development
2-2.5 ml OD PO if indicated (rapid growth phase, prematurity, low intake)

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 drops TDS PO only if symptomatic colic, short duration only

notes: Preventive pediatric care protocol. Ensure exclusive breastfeeding till 6 months, feed every 2-3 hours, proper burping. Monthly growth monitoring (weight, length, head circumference) on WHO charts. Follow National Immunization Schedule. Monitor developmental milestones (social smile, head control, activity). Adequate sunlight exposure. Avoid overcrowding.
source: IAP Preventive Pediatric Care 2024, WHO Infant Care Guidelines, NNF (National Neonatology Forum) India
guideline_changes: IAP 2024: Vitamin D 400 IU/day universally recommended from birth; iron supplementation from 6 weeks for LBW/preterm, 6 months for term; emphasis on developmental milestone screening at every well-baby visit using validated tools. WHO 2024 growth standards reaffirmed.
counselling: Exclusive breastfeeding for first 6 months is the best nutrition
Feed baby every 2-3 hours and burp properly after each feed
Give Vitamin D drops daily - essential for strong bones
Give iron drops daily to prevent anemia
Bring child for monthly weight check and vaccination as per schedule
Report if poor feeding, excessive crying, fever, vomiting, or delayed milestones
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric specialist if failure to thrive, weight faltering across 2 centile lines, delayed milestones, congenital anomaly suspected, or recurrent infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute illness with danger signs (poor feeding, lethargy, fever in neonate, respiratory distress, severe dehydration), or severe failure to thrive requiring nutritional rehabilitation

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Screen for anemia at 6 months and 12 months

investigations.name: Serum Vitamin D
investigations.indication: If clinical signs of rickets or risk factors

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or refusal to feed
Fever in a newborn or young infant

first_line_drugs.notes: Explain to parents: common condition, self-limiting (peaks at 6-8 weeks), resolves by 3-4 months
0.3 ml (as per preparation) TDS PO after feeds, preferred for gas relief and safety
Proper latching technique, burping after every feed, avoid overfeeding

first_line_drugs.drug: Supplements (Multivitamin Drops + Vitamin D3 400 IU + Iron if indicated)
first_line_drugs.notes: As per standard practice; Multivitamin drops + Vitamin D3 400 IU daily + Iron supplementation if indicated

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 drops TDS PO (morning, evening, night), short duration only, avoid routine long-term use
Apply 2-3 times/day for oral/gum care, only if associated irritability

notes: Reassurance is the most important intervention. Infantile colic is self-limiting, peaks at 6-8 weeks, resolves by 3-4 months. Non-pharmacological measures: gentle abdominal massage, warm compress, carrying/rocking, white noise. Ensure proper latching, burping after every feed, avoid overfeeding. Breastfeeding mothers should avoid excess tea/coffee, spicy food, gas-forming foods. Simethicone preferred over dicyclomine for safety.
source: Rome IV Criteria (2016), IAP Infant Care Guidelines, ESPGHAN Probiotic Working Group
guideline_changes: Rome IV criteria (2016) remain current standard; Lactobacillus reuteri DSM 17938 supported by recent meta-analyses as effective probiotic for breastfed colicky infants; no new 2024-2025 Rome criteria revision. Reassurance and parental support remain cornerstone.
counselling: Colic is very common and not dangerous - it will resolve by 3-4 months
Gentle tummy massage and warm compress provide relief
Ensure proper breastfeeding technique with burping after every feed
Avoid overfeeding the baby
Breastfeeding mothers should avoid excess tea, coffee, spicy and gas-forming foods
Return if baby has poor feeding, vomiting, fever, blood in stool, or poor weight gain
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if colic persists beyond 4 months, failure to thrive, blood in stool, persistent vomiting, or suspected cow's milk protein allergy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed for colic. Admit only if poor feeding with dehydration, failure to thrive, suspected intestinal obstruction, or intussusception

investigations.name: Urine routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Rule out UTI as cause of excessive crying

investigations.name: Stool occult blood
investigations.indication: Suspected cow milk protein allergy if blood-streaked stools

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: If fever present or prolonged crying beyond 4 months to rule out infection

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor feeding or refusal to feed
Vomiting especially if bilious or forceful

first_line_drugs.notes: Keep baby head elevated ~30 degrees, hold upright 15-20 min after feeds, avoid immediate lying after feeding; first-line treatment
Small frequent feeds, avoid overfeeding, proper burping after feeds; reduces reflux episodes
1-2 mg/kg/day OD PO for 2-4 weeks trial, only if symptomatic GERD; not for simple GER
Thickened formula or rice cereal if bottle-fed; reduces regurgitation

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.3 mg/kg/dose TDS PO, short course, use cautiously in select cases

notes: Most infant GER is physiological and self-limiting, resolving by 12-18 months. Non-pharmacological measures are first-line: keep head elevated 30 degrees, hold upright 15-20 min after feeds, small frequent feeds, proper burping, avoid overfeeding. Thickened feeds (rice cereal) for bottle-fed infants reduce regurgitation. PPI only for symptomatic GERD with poor weight gain or complications. Do not medicate normal thriving babies with mild regurgitation.
source: ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN 2018 Pediatric GERD Clinical Practice Guidelines, IAP GI Protocol
guideline_changes: NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN 2018 GERD CPG (most recent): 4-8 weeks PPI trial for typical GERD symptoms only after dietary modifications fail in infants; avoid acid suppression for extraesophageal symptoms (cough, wheezing); no new 2024-2025 GERD guideline revision published.
counselling: Keep baby's head elevated after feeds and hold upright for 15-20 minutes
Give small frequent feeds and avoid overfeeding
Burp baby properly after every feed
Most babies outgrow reflux by 12-18 months - this is usually normal
Do not lay baby flat immediately after feeding
Return if baby has poor weight gain, persistent vomiting, blood in vomit, or choking episodes
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if poor weight gain despite treatment, persistent vomiting beyond 18 months, hematemesis, suspected aspiration, recurrent pneumonia, or Sandifer syndrome
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe GERD with failure to thrive, aspiration pneumonia, apparent life-threatening event (ALTE/BRUE), hematemesis, or severe dehydration from persistent vomiting

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Check for anemia if chronic GERD

investigations.name: Ultrasound abdomen
investigations.indication: Rule out pyloric stenosis or structural causes in persistent vomiting

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: If persistent vomiting to rule out hepatobiliary causes

warning_signs: Bluish discoloration of lips or tongue
Poor weight gain or failure to thrive
Persistent vomiting or blood in vomit
Severe irritability or refusal to feed
